Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 202km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (70kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 165km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 88 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 66min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 83 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 70min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 44min, 3rd: 56min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 203km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (72kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (35kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 60km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 92 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 205km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (69kg each) and 3 units of relief supply box (52kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 182km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 87 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 61min, 3rd: 75min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 192km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 98 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 35 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 35min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 59min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 224km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 8 units of construction material (76kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 208km waypoint, 4 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 46min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (76kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (5kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 89 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 64min, 3rd: 77min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 228km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (58kg each) and 8 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 30km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.
From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 65km waypoint, 8 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 96 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 65min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 199km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (80kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 91km waypoint, 1 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 96 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 66min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

- Water barrels: 4 units * 24kg/unit = 96kg

- Total initial cargo: 960kg + 96kg = 1056kg

The safety weight threshold is 700kg. Since 1056kg > 700kg, the cargo exceeds the threshold, so speed limits are reduced.

For Zone A, speed limit reduced by 22%; for Zone B, reduced by 25%.

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 202km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (65kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (10kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 52 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 52min, 2nd: 66min, 3rd: 80min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 194km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (54kg each) and 8 units of construction material (65kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 143km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 88 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 62min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (74kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (50kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 19%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 78km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 65min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 227km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of construction material (71kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (15kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 144km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 93 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 56min, 3rd: 72min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
resps:
I need to solve this boat navigation problem. Captain Han is transporting goods upstream for 227 km total. He starts at 8:00 AM with 13 units of construction material (71kg each) and 3 units of water barrels (15kg each). The boat speed in still water is 41 km/h, and there's a river current of 5 km/h. Zone A is the first 29 km with downstream current, so effective speed is boat speed plus current. Zone B is the rest with upstream current, so effective speed minus current.

First, I should calculate the cargo weight to see if it exceeds the safety threshold. Each construction material is 71kg, and there are 13 units. So 13 * 71 = let's see, 10*71=710, 3*71=213, total 710+213=923kg. Water barrels: 3 * 15 = 45kg. Total cargo: 923 + 45 = 968kg. Safety threshold is 800kg, so 968 > 800, yes, it exceeds. So the speed limits will be reduced.

For Zone A, speed limit is normally 31 km/h, but reduced by 22% because of cargo. 22% of 31 is 0.22*31=6.82, so 31 - 6.82 = 24.18 km/h. But since speed limits apply to effective speed, I need to be careful.

Similarly, Zone B normal speed limit 21 km/h, reduced by 27%. 27% of 21 is 0.27*21=5.67, so 21 - 5.67 = 15.33 km/h.

But these are the maximum allowed effective speeds. Also, there's congestion from 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM, which affects Zone B only, reducing speed limit by additional 23% during that time.

Now, the trip has two zones: Zone A 29 km, then Zone B for the remaining distance. Total distance 227 km, so Zone B is 227 - 29 = 198 km? But there's a waypoint at 144 km where he unloads some cargo.

From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (51kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (62kg each). The boat can travel at 44km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 33km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 19%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 26km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 83 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 48min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 19%.
From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 195km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 96 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 56min, 3rd: 70min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 214km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (65kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (48kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 52km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 87 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 12 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 63min, 3rd: 75min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 227km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 8 units of construction material (56kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 30km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 117km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 46min, 3rd: 60min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 98 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 80min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 195km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (69kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (56kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.
From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 84km waypoint, 4 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 38 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 38min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 68min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 215km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 3 units of construction material (63kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.
From 8:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 144km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 85 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 33 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 33min, 2nd: 49min, 3rd: 65min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 13 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 65min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 91km waypoint, 5 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 97 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 69min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 221km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (79kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (59kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.
From 8:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 84km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 92 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 79min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
resps:

**Step 1: Determine the cargo weight and apply cargo regulations.**

- Construction material: 16 units * 79 kg each = 16 * 79 = 1264 kg

- Relief supply boxes: 5 units * 59 kg each = 5 * 59 = 295 kg

- Total initial cargo weight: 1264 + 295 = 1559 kg

Safety weight threshold is 1000 kg. Since 1559 kg > 1000 kg, the cargo exceeds the limit, so speed limits are reduced.

- Zone A speed limit reduction: 18%

- Zone B speed limit reduction: 23%

- Zone A: 31 km/h

- Zone B: 19 km/h

After cargo reduction:

- Zone A effective speed limit: 31 * (1 - 0.18) = 31 * 0.82 = 25.42 km/h

- Zone B effective speed limit: 19 * (1 - 0.23) = 19 * 0.77 = 14.63 km/h

But these are speed limits, not the actual speeds. The boat can go faster, but it must obey the speed limits. Also, we have to consider the current.

The effective speed depends on whether the current is with or against the boat.

In Zone A, current is downstream, so effective speed = boat speed + current speed.

But the speed limit applies to this effective speed.

- Zone A: first 26 km, current downstream, speed limit 31 km/h effective (after cargo reduction, it's 25.42 km/h)

After cargo reduction, the speed limit is reduced, so for Zone A, the effective speed must not exceed 25.42 km/h.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 226km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (68kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 44km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 154km waypoint, 5 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 89 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 57min, 3rd: 72min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
resps:
First, I need to find the total travel time for Cho to reach the final destination, including mandatory rest stops. The total distance is 226 km. The journey is divided into zones: the first 26 km is Zone A, and the rest is Zone B, which is 226 - 26 = 200 km.

Now, let's look at the cargo. Cho starts with 17 units of construction material at 68 kg each and 5 units of medical kits at 12 kg each.

Calculate the initial cargo weight:

- Construction material: 17 units * 68 kg/unit = 1156 kg

- Medical kits: 5 units * 12 kg/unit = 60 kg

- Total initial weight: 1156 + 60 = 1216 kg

The safety weight threshold is 900 kg. Since 1216 kg > 900 kg, the cargo exceeds the threshold, so we need to reduce the speed limits.

For Zone A, speed limit is reduced by 18%. The base speed limit for Zone A is 32 km/h.

Reduced by 18%: 32 * (1 - 0.18) = 32 * 0.82 = 26.24 km/h

Similarly, for Zone B, base speed limit is 19 km/h, reduced by 27%: 19 * (1 - 0.27) = 19 * 0.73 = 13.87 km/h

But these are effective speeds after accounting for current, and the speed limits apply to effective speed. Also, the current affects the boat speed.

The boat speed in still water is 44 km/h.

In Zone A, current is downstream, so effective speed = boat speed + current = 44 + 5 = 49 km/h, but this is without speed limits. The speed limit is 32 km/h normally, but reduced to 26.24 km/h due to cargo.

The speed limit applies to the effective speed, meaning the boat cannot go faster than the speed limit in that zone, considering the current.

Also, during congestion period from 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM, in Zone B, there's an additional reduction of 24% on top of the already reduced speed limit.

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 222km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of relief supply box (65kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (10kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 132km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 93 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 37 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 37min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 69min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 217km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 8 units of construction material (59kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.
From 8:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 182km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 97 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 54 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 54min, 2nd: 69min, 3rd: 84min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 10 units of construction material (74kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (37kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.
From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 143km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 90 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 62min, 3rd: 76min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
